actions.thought: I am in the Small Blind (Button) heads-up with 5h 3s, which is a very weak off-suit hand (bottom 10% of hands). While position is advantageous in heads-up play, 53o has poor playability and low equity against a random hand. At 100bb deep, there is no need to get involved with trash hands early in the match. Folding is the standard play to avoid bleeding chips with negative expected value holdings.

Qh Kh is an extremely strong hand in heads-up play, ranking in the top tier of starting hands. Being on the button provides a significant positional advantage for the remainder of the hand. I will raise to 300 to build a pot with my premium hand and exert pressure on the big blind, a standard opening size in this structure.

I hold Pocket Jacks [Jc, Jd] in the Big Blind. In heads-up play, the Button is the Small Blind, so my opponent acts last post-flop. Facing a standard 3bb open (300) from the Button, I have a premium hand that plays best aggressively, especially since I will be out of position post-flop. Flat calling would allow the opponent to realize equity cheaply and puts me in a tough spot on overcard flops. A 3-bet for value is the standard play here to charge worse pairs, Ax, and broadways. A sizing of roughly 3.5x to 4x the open is appropriate given the 100bb depth and being out of position. Raising to 1100 is a strong, standard size.

Qh Kh is a premium starting hand in heads-up play with approximately 65% equity against a wide range. My opponent's 3-bet to 1100 is a standard sizing. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to use my positional advantage (being on the button) for the rest of the hand and see a flop. This keeps the pot controlled and allows me to see how the board develops without committing too much of my stack immediately against potential monster hands like AA or KK, while still having excellent equity against most of the opponent's range.

The flop comes Qc 3c Kd. This texture connects very well with the opponent's 3-bet calling range, which consists heavily of broadway hands (KJs, KQs, QJs, AQ, etc.) and pocket pairs. While I have range advantage in terms of having AA/AK/KK/QQ, my specific hand (JJ) has been counterfeited by two overcards. I am out of position and likely behind any Kx or Qx hand. Betting here achieves little; worse hands (like lower pairs) might fold, but better hands will certainly call or raise. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce bluffs from missed draws or lower pairs, and realize my equity (including a gutshot straight draw). I have the Jc which provides a blocker to flush draws. The standard play with underpairs on wet, high-card boards in a 3-bet pot OOP is to check.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Queens) on a board of Kc-Qc-3c. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked to me. While the board has a flush draw, my hand is extremely strong. Checking back would risk giving a free card to a club draw or a gutshot. I will bet for value, targeting hands like AK, AA, or even a strong Jack-Ten or club draw that my opponent might have 3-bet with. A bet of 1100 (half the pot) is a standard size to build the pot and protect my hand equity.

The board texture (Kd Qc 3c) is very bad for Pocket Jacks in a 3-bet pot, as it connects heavily with the opponent's calling range (Kx, Qx, broadway draws). Although I have the Jc which blocks some flush and straight draws, this actually reduces the number of semi-bluffs the opponent might have, weighting their betting range more towards value hands that crush me (Kx, Qx, Sets). Facing a half-pot bet while out of position, I am likely drawing to 2 outs against value, and I will face difficult decisions on future streets even against draws. Preserving the stack early in the match by surrendering this poor post-flop setup is the prudent play.

In Heads-Up play, King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the Button. I have position post-flop and an effective stack of 88 big blinds. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or build a pot where I have a range advantage. Folding is too passive for this holding, and while limping is an option, raising to 2bb (200) is the standard aggressive play to apply pressure to the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. Pro has min-raised to 200 from the Button. Given the 4:1 pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot) and being heads-up, K5o is a mandatory defend in this spot. I will call to see the flop and evaluate from there, especially since I have a stack advantage.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. This board favors the Button's range, which likely contains many Ace-x combinations. By checking, I can see how 'Pro' chooses to act and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheap turn. Leading out here would likely only be called by better hands.

The flop comes 5s 6d Ac. As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace heavily favors my range, allowing me to represent a strong hand. However, with King-Seven offsuit, I have only King-high and no significant draws. While a continuation bet is standard on Ace-high boards to fold out equity, my hand has some showdown value against missed connectors. Additionally, the opponent showed aggression in the previous hand, suggesting they might check-raise or float aggressively. Checking back controls the pot size, avoids getting blown off my equity by a check-raise, and allows me to see a free turn to potentially hit a King or Seven. It also protects my checking range.

I have middle pair (fives) with a King kicker. On the flop, both I and 'Pro' checked, which suggests 'Pro' likely does not have a strong Ace. The turn 9s is a relatively blank card, though it could have helped some of Pro's drawing range. Since I am out of position and my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to build a large pot, checking again is the most prudent play. This allows me to keep the pot small and potentially induce a bluff from a weaker hand or see a cheap river card.

The turn brings the 9s, putting a flush draw on the board. My hand (King-Seven offsuit) is currently just King-high. My check back on the flop capped my range somewhat, but the opponent's double check signals they likely don't have a strong Ace. However, they could easily hold a pair of 5s, 6s, 9s, or a low pocket pair which currently beats me. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, but it's unlikely to fold out a pair for a small size, and a large size is risky in a small pot. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (hitting a King or Seven on the river might win) for free and keeps the pot manageable. I will take the free card.

The river pairs the Ace, which is generally a good card for my hand (Two Pair, Aces and Fives) because it reduces the likelihood that 'Pro' holds an Ace, especially after checking both the flop and turn. My hand has significant showdown value in a small, checked-down pot. By checking, I allow 'Pro' to potentially bluff with air or check back weaker hands that I already beat. There is little value in betting as most hands that call me would likely have me beaten (like a 6, 9, or a better pocket pair), whereas checking ensures I reach showdown for free.

On the river, the board is 5s 6d Ac 9s Ad. The pot is 400. The opponent has checked to me for the third time (Flop, Turn, River). This triple check line indicates significant weakness, likely a missed draw, weak pair, or total air. My hand is Kh 7d. With the pair of Aces on the board, my five-card hand is A-A-K-9-6. This beats all opponent's air hands (Queen-high and lower) but loses to any pocket pair, any connected pair (5x, 6x, 9x), or a King with a better kicker. Betting here would unlikely get called by worse hands (except maybe a suspicious Q-high), and it is questionable whether better hands like a small pair (e.g., 22-44 or a weak 5x) would fold in a limped/small pot given my passive line post-flop. Checking back allows me to take my showdown value against missed draws and lower high-cards without risking chips in a spot where I have little fold equity against made hands.
Holding Ace-four offsuit on the button, this is a standard opening range hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h7h, a suited one-gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop. The opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 3bb (300). In Heads-Up play, defending the Big Blind with suited connectors/gappers is standard due to the wide range of the opener. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity. 97s has sufficient equity against a typical button opening range to justify a call, despite being out of position post-flop.

I hold 9h7h on a board of 3s Ad Td. I have completely missed the flop, having no pair and no meaningful draw (backdoor straight/flush possibilities are negligible). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is the standard line. The Ace-high texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, so leading out (donk betting) would be ill-advised. I will check and almost certainly fold to any continuation bet.

I have top pair on a board with an Ace, a Ten, and a Trey. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Pro checked to me, I will place a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (50% of the pot) is appropriate here to extract value from weaker hands like a Ten, middle pairs, or diamond draws, while also protecting my hand against those draws. Checking back would risk giving a free card on a relatively wet board.

I hold 9h7h on a board of 3s Ad Td. I have no pair and no draw. The opponent has continuation bet 300 into a pot of 600. This Ace-high, flush-draw-heavy texture connects very well with the pre-flop raiser's range. My hand has negligible equity and I am out of position. I am not getting the correct odds to call, nor do I have a strategic reason to bluff raise on this texture. The disciplined play is to fold.

I hold 5d 4c in the Small Blind (Button). In Heads-Up play, I have position post-flop, but 54o is a very weak hand, situated near the bottom of the playable range. While I could limp to see a cheap flop in position, the hand has poor playability and low equity against the Big Blind's range. Given my stack deficit and the early stage of the match, I prefer to fold this weak holding rather than invest chips with negative expected value. Preserving my stack for better spots is the prudent play here.

I have been dealt Ace-King suited (AcKc) on the button. This is a premium starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure, while maintaining my positional advantage throughout the hand.

The opponent has raised to 3x (300 chips). I hold T7o in the Big Blind. While heads-up requires defending wide, T7o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Against a 3x raise, the price to call is not compelling enough for this specific holding, as realizing equity will be difficult. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I hold 7c 5h on the Button (SB). This is a weak unsuited gapper (75o). In Heads-Up play, this hand falls below the standard opening range for raising and is marginal even for limping. Given the opponent has shown aggression in previous hands, entering the pot with such a weak hand out of position pre-flop (though in position post-flop) is likely -EV. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I have Ace-King offsuit on the button in a heads-up match, which is a premium starting hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and capitalize on my positional advantage. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to aggression in previous hands, and AKo has significant equity against a wide range of hands that might call.

I hold Qd 4h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. My opponent has been opening to 300 consistently. Although I'm getting 2:1 pot odds requiring ~33% equity, Q4o is a weak, uncoordinated hand that plays poorly out of position. It suffers from reverse implied odds and is easily dominated. Against an aggressive opponent who c-bets frequently, calling here is likely -EV. Folding is the prudent decision to preserve chips.

I hold ATo on the button, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the random range of the Big Blind. Raising allows me to take the initiative and play the pot in position post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is a standard size at 80BB effective stack depth to extract value and deny free equity to the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit. The opponent (Pro) has min-raised to 200. In a heads-up situation, Q8o is well above the defensive threshold against a min-raise, offering favorable pot odds of 3:1. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially strong range. Based on the previous hands, Pro has been playing relatively tight-aggressive, and defending here is the standard play.

I am first to act on a very connected flop of 9d-6d-7c. I hold Queen-high with an inside straight draw (gutshot to a Ten). Given the board texture and the fact that I called a pre-flop raise, checking to the aggressor is the most standard and balanced play. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts to this board before committing more chips with a speculative hand.

I hold AhTc on a 9d-6d-7c flop. This board texture is very wet and connects well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). Although I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8), my hand is vulnerable to a check-raise, which would force me to fold my equity. Betting might only fold out hands I am already beating (like King-high) while getting called by anything better. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, potentially hit an Ace, Ten, or Eight on the turn, and keep the pot small.

On the turn 4d, the board is now 9d-6d-7c-4d, presenting three diamonds and several straight possibilities. I hold the 8d, which gives me a flush draw and an open-ended straight draw (6-7-8-9). Since the flop went check-check, Pro likely has a marginal hand or overcards. I will check here to see if Pro bets; given my significant equity with both flush and straight draws, I can comfortably call a bet to see the river, or take a free card if Pro checks behind.

On the turn, the board becomes even more coordinated with the 4d, bringing a third diamond and completing several straight draws. I still hold Ace-high and have picked up no flush draw myself. The opponent has checked again, signaling weakness or a trap. Betting here is risky; worse hands (like King-high) will fold, and better hands (pairs, flushes, straights) will call or raise, putting me in a difficult spot. My hand has some showdown value and I still have outs to improve (an 8 for a straight, or an Ace/Ten for top pair). Checking back controls the pot size and ensures I see the river for free to realize my equity.

On the river King of Hearts, the board is 9d-6d-7c-4d-Kh. I have Queen-high (Qs 8d) and no showdown value. The turn brought a third diamond and several straight possibilities. Both players checked the flop and the turn, which suggests a weak range for the opponent, but also means the pot is small (400). While I could attempt a bluff to represent the straight, the flush, or the King, my hand has zero equity if called, and checking is the most disciplined play to avoid losing more chips with a hand that failed to improve. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

The river is the King of Hearts, completing the board as 9d 6d 7c 4d Kh. The opponent has checked to me on all three streets (flop, turn, river). The board is somewhat coordinated with three diamonds and straight possibilities (any 5 or 8 makes a straight). I hold Ace-high. Betting here has little value: worse hands (like Queen-high or lower) will fold, and better hands (any pair, of which there are many possible on this board) will likely call. While Ace-high has decent showdown value in a limped/checked-down pot heads-up, turning it into a bluff is unnecessary as I likely beat the opponent's missed draws. Checking back realizes my equity and ensures I don't lose more chips to a trap or a weak pair.

Holding 7-5 suited on the button in a heads-up match is a strong candidate for an open. I have a significant chip lead and position on my opponent. Based on the previous hands, Pro has shown some tightness pre-flop and post-flop. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and build the pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

Opponent raises to 300 (3BB) from the button. I hold 3h 4s, which is a bottom-tier off-suit connector. Even in heads-up play, this hand is too weak to defend against a 3x raise out of position. I need 33% equity to call (200 into 600), and this hand likely falls short of that against a solid opening range, plus it suffers from poor playability post-flop. Folding is the correct standard play.

I hold QJo on the Button, which is a strong hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. My opponent has shown aggression but also defends their blind. Raising allows me to build the pot for value, seize the initiative, and deny free equity to weak hands. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate given the 81bb effective stack depth.

Pro has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. Holding 6c 5s in the Big Blind, I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (100 more into a 300 pot). While 65o is a weak hand, it is sufficient to defend against a min-raise in a heads-up situation to see a flop.

On a dry board of 5h-2d-2s, I have flopped top pair with a 6 kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Standard play as the Big Blind defender is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to allow them to continuation bet with their air or overcards. Leading out here would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like pocket pairs 66+ or a Deuce) and fold out the hands I want to extract value from or induce bluffs from. I will check and see how Pro reacts.

I hold QJo on a dry, paired board of 5-2-2. While I have a range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, this texture doesn't hit my hand specifically, though I have two overcards. The opponent (Flash) has shown aggression post-flop in previous hands (e.g., check-raising Hand #2). Betting folds out worse hands (like T-high or random trash) but isolates me against better hands (pairs, Ace-high) or induces a check-raise bluff which would force me to fold decent equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot small, and lets me potentially call a bet on the turn or bet for value later if I improve. Queen-high has enough showdown value to check here.

On the turn, the board is 5h-2d-2s-4s. I have top pair with a six kicker. Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value like Ace-high or a weak pocket pair, or a total air hand. Since I likely have the best hand, I will lead out with a bet of 200 (half the pot) to get value from hands that might call such as high card hands or to deny equity to overcards.

Pre-flop, raising QJo on the Button is standard to isolate and build a pot with a strong heads-up hand. I checked back the flop for pot control, as the paired low board (5-2-2) is dry and Q-high has reasonable showdown value. The turn brings a 4, connecting straight draws and a flush draw. The opponent leads out for half-pot. This bet polarizes their range to trips (2x), pairs (4x, 5x), or semi-bluffs. With only 6 clean outs to top pair (approx. 13% equity) and facing pot odds of 3:1 (requiring 25%), I am not getting the direct price to call. Floating with just Q-high against an aggressive opponent who may barrel the river is risky. Folding preserves the stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8c 2c. Although this is a weak hand, the opponent has shown a high frequency of folding to pre-flop raises in earlier hands. I will put pressure on the Big Blind by raising to 300, looking to take the pot down immediately. If called, I have a suited hand with some post-flop playability, though I will need to be cautious.

I am in the Big Blind with Th 7s. My opponent 'Flash' has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. This 3x sizing worsens my pot odds compared to a min-raise, requiring me to realize 33% equity out of position. I held this exact hand (Th 7s) in Hand #6 facing the same action and chose to fold. T7o is a marginal hand that often suffers from reverse implied odds and is difficult to play profitably out of position against a 3x open. Given the consistency of the opponent's sizing and my previous decision, maintaining a folding range here is prudent.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, Q9o is a solid hand to open from the Button (Small Blind). It falls well within the top 50-60% of starting hands. The opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to defend the Big Blind by calling min-raises rather than 3-betting aggressively (observed in Hands 3, 9, and 11). Therefore, a min-raise allows me to maintain the initiative, play the pot in position post-flop, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop if the opponent folds. Folding is too conservative, and raising is generally preferred over limping to build the pot with a decent holding.

I have Qs 7s in the Big Blind against a button raise to 200 from Pro. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring only 25% equity to call. Q7s is well above this threshold against a standard button opening range. Given Pro's history of folding strong hands like AK pre-flop and folding easily post-flop, calling to see a flop and potentially outplaying him is the most efficient move.

The flop comes Jack-Jack-Deuce, a paired board that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, but also one that is hard to hit. I have Queen-high with no draw. Given Pro's history of extreme passivity and willingness to fold strong hands (like AK and KQ) under pressure, I will check first to see his reaction. If he checks back, I can try to take the pot on a later street. If he bets, I will likely fold as I have zero equity here, though his high fold-to-aggression frequency suggests that leading out or check-raising might be profitable in the future.

The flop falls Jc Js 2h, a paired and very dry texture. As the pre-flop raiser, I have a range advantage (holding all strong Jx and overpairs), but my specific holding of Qd 9s is a marginal hand with some showdown value (beating 78s, T9s, etc.) but drawing thin against a Jack or a pocket pair. 

In Hand #11, on a similar paired board (5-2-2), I checked back and faced a turn lead which forced a fold. While c-betting small (range bet) is a viable strategy here to deny equity to random air, Q9o specifically benefits from checking back to realize equity and keep the opponent's range wide (including hands I beat). Betting likely folds out worse hands while getting called by better (Ax, Kx, pairs, Jx). I will check back to control the pot size and evaluate the turn.

I have improved to a pair of Sevens on the turn. On the flop, Pro checked back on a Jack-Jack-Deuce board, which suggests he likely doesn't have a Jack or a strong pocket pair. Given Pro's demonstrated history of extreme passivity and his tendency to fold even strong hands like top pair to aggression (as seen in Hands #2 and #4), a lead here is likely to take the pot immediately. In Hand #11, a similar turn lead on a paired board successfully forced a fold from Pro. I will bet 200 to either take the pot now or get value from his high-card hands if he decides to call.

On the turn, the board is [Jc Js 2h 7c]. The opponent leads for 200 (half pot) after I checked back the flop. This mirrors the action in Hand #11 where the opponent took the same line on a similar paired board structure, and I folded Q-high. My hand (Q9o) is currently just Queen-high. While the opponent's lead could be a probe with a wide range attempting to exploit my show of weakness, I am near the bottom of my range. I have plenty of better hands to defend with, such as Ax, Kx, or any pair (2x, 7x, pocket pairs). Calling with Q-high requires me to be right that he is bluffing very frequently, and I will likely face a difficult decision on the river. Given the pot odds and my relative hand strength, the disciplined play is to fold, respecting the turn lead as I did previously.

I am in the Small Blind with T4o. Looking at the match history, the opponent (Pro) has been folding their Big Blind to my 3x raises quite frequently (Hands 6, 8, 10, and 12). While T4o is a weak starting hand, the high frequency of folds from the opponent makes a raise profitable as a steal attempt. I will continue with my established sizing of 300 chips.

The opponent is opening 3x (300 chips) from the button, a sizing they have used consistently in every button opportunity shown so far. While my hand 97o has enough raw equity (~43%) against a random hand to technically satisfy the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400 requires 33%), playing this hand out of position against a 3x raise is difficult. I will likely under-realize my equity due to the position disadvantage and the marginal nature of the hand. I have previously folded slightly stronger hands like T7o to this same action. Therefore, folding is the prudent long-term play to avoid bleeding chips in low-EV spots.

I hold King-Queen suited (Kc Qc) on the Button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent has defended their big blind frequently against my raises in previous hands (Hands 3, 9, 11, 13). Raising builds the pot for value and takes the initiative. A standard min-raise to 200 is consistent with my previous bet sizing and balances my range.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 7s, a marginal but playable hand in a heads-up situation. The opponent, Pro, has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. The pot is 300, and it only costs me 100 more to call. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the wide range of hands a Button can raise with, calling to see a flop is a standard play here to defend my blind. I have a significant chip lead and can afford to see if I connect with the board.

I have flopped middle pair with my seven on a board containing an Ace. In a heads-up pot, middle pair is a relatively strong holding, but since the opponent was the pre-flop aggressor and the board contains an Ace (which is well within their range), I will check to them to see how they proceed. I intend to call a standard continuation bet to see how the turn develops.

I raised pre-flop with KcQc and the flop is As 7d 5c. This is a very dry, Ace-high board that significantly favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. Looking at previous hands (Hands 3, 9, 11, 13), I have checked back every single flop when in position, allowing my opponent to realize equity or bluff the turn (as they did in Hands 11 and 13). To prevent becoming predictable and to capitalize on the Ace which hits my perceived range, I must make a continuation bet here. A small bet is sufficient to fold out hands that missed and deny equity to random holdings. My hand has some backdoor equity and showdown value, but I prefer to take the pot down now or define the hand strength.

I have flopped middle pair (sevens) on a board with an Ace. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a 400-chip pot. In a heads-up match, middle pair is a strong enough hand to continue against a small bet, especially since the pot odds (3.6:1) are favorable. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Aces) and fold out worse ones, so I will call to keep their bluffs in and see how the turn card changes the board texture.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board of As-7d-5c-6h. My opponent was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop. The turn 6h doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does add some straight possibilities. I want to keep the pot small and reach showdown with my pair, or catch a lucky 8 on the river. Checking is the best option here to see how the opponent reacts; if they check behind, my sevens are likely good, and if they bet, I can evaluate the sizing to decide on a call.

The turn brings a 6h, making the board 5c As 7d 6h. This card connects the board significantly (5-6-7), which heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range (containing 8x, 4x, two pairs, or sets) over my high-card heavy range. Since the opponent called my flop c-bet on the Ace-high board, they likely hold a piece of the board (an Ace, a pair, or a straight draw) that has now improved or gained equity. My King-high has little fold equity against an Ace or a straight, and betting now would likely only build a pot I am losing. Checking back allows me to realize any equity I have (hitting a King or Queen on the river) and keeps the pot small, avoiding a check-raise.

On the river, the board has completed a straight (5-6-7-8). With the Nine of Diamonds in my hand, I have a nine-high straight (5-6-7-8-9). While a ten-high straight is mathematically possible if the opponent holds 10-9, my hand is near the top of my range here. The opponent checked back on the turn, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value like a weak Ace or a pair that didn't want to build a huge pot. I will lead out with a value bet of 500 into the 700-chip pot to extract value from those hands or a lower straight (using a 4).

The river brings an 8h, completing a four-card straight on the board (5-6-7-8). This runout heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many 9x and 4x hands making a straight, as well as random pairs (8x, 7x, 6x, 5x) and Aces that called the flop. My King-high has virtually no showdown value against an opponent who called a flop bet and is now leading into me on a scary board. My check-back on the turn signaled weakness, and the opponent is capitalizing on it, likely with a value hand or a strong bluff that still beats K-high. I cannot profitably call or bluff raise here.

I am on the button with J4o. In a heads-up match, especially against an opponent who has been playing relatively tight and folding to many pre-flop raises, raising from the button is a standard strategy to exert pressure and win the blinds. Even with a mediocre hand like J4o, a standard 3x raise is often profitable.

I hold Kd5d in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My opponent, Flash, has been opening very frequently (Hands 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16). Against a wide opening range, K5s has solid equity and plays well enough post-flop to justify a call. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a pot of 600 total, requiring ~33% equity). Folding would be too tight given the aggressor's frequency, and 3-betting is risky out of position with this specific holding. I will flat call to see a flop.

I hold Kd5d on a flop of 7h Js Qh. This texture connects very well with the pre-flop aggressor's wide opening range (Queens, Jacks, broadway draws, hearts). I have King-high with no pair and no meaningful draw. Being out of position with very low equity, leading out is not profitable. The standard play is to check to the raiser. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold given the lack of potential, but for now, checking costs nothing.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on a board of Qh-Js-7h. The pot is 600 chips. Pro has checked to me. While my Jack is a decent hand, the board is quite connected with straight and flush draws, and there is an overcard (the Queen). Given the current stacks and the 'to call' amount being only 100 more, calling is a very standard and profitable play here.

On this J-7-4 rainbow flop, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet into the pre-flop aggressor. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, planning to potentially call a small bet or fold if the pressure is too high, as this board doesn't connect well with my specific hole cards.

I hold bottom pair (4s) with a King kicker on a J-7-4 board with two spades. Checking back allows me to pot control, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets or call a bet on the turn. On the turn, the board now shows three spades with the deuce of spades. I have Ace-high with no spades. Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a lack of a strong hand or a desire to control the pot. However, with the flush draw completing, I am out of position and my hand has limited equity if called. I will check here to see if Pro checks behind, allowing me to potentially see a cheap showdown or a favorable river card. If Pro bets, I will likely fold as Ace-high without a flush draw is difficult to continue with on this texture.

I hold a pair of Fours and the second nut flush draw (Ks) on a board of [4s 7d Js 2s]. After checking back the flop, the turn brings a third spade, completing possible flushes. My hand has significant showdown value and drawing potential. Betting risks facing a check-raise which would put me in a difficult spot, potentially forcing me to fold a hand with good equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit a flush or two pair on the river, or get to showdown cheaply with my current pair.

On the river, the board has completed a four-flush with the Queen of Spades. I have Ace-high with the Ace of Hearts and no spades. The opponent, Pro, checked back on both the flop and the turn after raising pre-flop, which suggests they likely have a marginal hand or are giving up. If Pro bets, the four-flush makes it too risky to call with just Ace-high, and I will likely fold.

The river card is the Queen of Spades (Qs), putting four spades on the board (4s, Js, 2s, Qs). Holding the King of Spades (Ks), I now have the second-nut flush, losing only to the Ace of Spades (As). The opponent has checked to me on the flop, turn, and now river, which is a passive line that suggests they likely do not hold the nut flush, as they would typically bet or check-raise for value by now. I am well ahead of the opponent's range, which includes lower flushes (e.g., Ts, 9s, 8s) and potentially top pairs (Qx) or two pairs that might hero call. Checking back would miss significant value. A value bet of roughly half to 60% of the pot is appropriate to extract value from worse hands without overcommitting against a trap.

On a board with four spades (4s, 7d, Js, 2s, Qs), any single spade in the opponent's hand makes a flush. I hold Ace-high (Ah, 6c) with no spades. Given that Pro raised pre-flop, their range includes many hands that contain a spade or have made a pair. I cannot profitably call here with just Ace-high.

I have 5s 4s on the button, which is a strong speculative hand in heads-up play. Given my significant chip lead and the opponent's tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands, I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with A3o facing a 3bb (300) open from the button. In heads-up play, A3o is a respectable hand with decent equity (around 50%) against a wide opening range. The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), requiring 33% equity. While the hand plays somewhat poorly post-flop out of position, it is too strong to fold pre-flop given the wide ranges in heads-up poker. 3-betting is an option as a bluff with an Ace blocker, but flat calling is the standard lower-variance play to keep the opponent's range wide and see a flop.

On a paired board of J-J-8 rainbow, my Ace-high (A3o) actually has decent showdown value against a wide button opening range that contains many missed random hands. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as I fold out worse hands (like K-high) and get action from better hands (pairs). Checking allows the opponent to bluff or check back, keeping the pot small which favors my marginal showdown value hand. I will check and evaluate based on their action.

On a paired board of J-J-8, I have 5-high with a backdoor spade flush draw. My opponent checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. The opponent c-bets 300 (half pot) on a paired board of [8h Js Jc]. While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, A3o is near the bottom of my range for continuing. I am on the Button with A3o. In heads-up play, any Ace is a reasonably strong starting hand. I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop, which gives me 3-to-1 pot odds. King-high hands have reasonable equity against a standard button opening range, making this a clear defend. I will call to see the flop.

I have missed the flop completely with my King-high. The board features an Ace, which is a significant part of the pre-flop raiser's range. I will check to the aggressor and evaluate their response. There is no reason to lead out here without a piece of the board or a strong draw.

I hold top pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (3) on a board of [6c Ac 8s]. While I have a strong hand for heads-up play, my kicker makes this a pot control situation. Betting risks isolating me against better Aces (domination) or getting check-raised. The board has a flush draw, but giving one free card is acceptable to protect my hand from domination issues. I plan to call a turn bet or bet for value if checked to again.

On the Turn, the board now has three clubs. After Pro checked back on the Ace-high flop, it's possible they are weak, but my King-high also has very little showdown value. On the turn, I have improved to top two pair (Aces and Threes). However, the 3c brings a third club to the board, completing potential flushes. I am facing a bet of 200 into a 400 pot on the turn. The board is Ace-high and now has three clubs. I am on the Button with Ts 4s. I am in the Big Blind with 8d 9d (suited connector), facing a standard 3x raise from the Button (Flash). 98s is a very playable hand heads-up and defends well against a wide opening range. I am out of position on a 5s-7s-3d flop with 8d-9d. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) and two overcards to the board. On this 5-7-3 rainbow board, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) and a backdoor spade flush draw with my Ts 4s. I raised pre-flop and Pro checked the flop. I hold 8d9d on a 5s-7s-3d flop. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) and two overcards to the board, plus a backdoor flush draw. On the turn, the 9c significantly improves my hand to Top Pair (Nines) with an 8 kicker, while retaining the gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). The board is now 5s 7s 3d 9c. After Pro checks the turn, I have a great opportunity to semi-bluff. My Ts4s has developed significant equity with both a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 total outs). On the turn, the 9c gives me top pair with a decent kicker (8) and maintains my gutshot straight draw. The opponent bets 600 into a pot of 1200 (half-pot), offering 3:1 odds. On the River, the Queen of Diamonds arrives, making the board 5s 7s 3d 9c Qd. This card is an overcard to my pair of Nines, downgrading my hand to second pair. Checking is the correct play to induce a check-back from weaker hands or missed draws, or to evaluate a bet from the opponent based on sizing. My goal is to get to showdown as cheaply as possible.

On this river, the Queen of Diamonds is an overcard that doesn't significantly help my specific hand (Ten-high). I have missed both my spade flush draw and my gutshot straight draw. I am on the Button with J8o. I am holding Nine-Deuce offsuit in the Small Blind. While I have the advantage of being on the Button and acting last post-flop, this hand is extremely weak with poor playability. I am on the button with QcTc, a strong suited connector. My standard opening raise size in this tournament has been 300, and this hand definitely warrants a raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and equity.

I hold Kh 4h on a flop of 3d 9h Ad. My opponent, the pre-flop raiser, has bet 300 (half pot) into a pot of 600. I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. In heads-up play, any Ace is a strong starting hand. Looking at the history, Pro folds frequently to a standard 3x raise. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or play a strong hand in position if called.

I hold King-Ace offsuit (KAo) in the Big Blind, a premium hand. My opponent, Flash, has been opening frequently (3x to 300) from the Button/Small Blind. I raised to 300 with Ace-Trey offsuit, a standard opening move from the button to exploit Pro's high fold-to-steal frequency. However, Pro has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. I am in the Small Blind with [8h 2s], which is a statistically very weak hand (trash). With 81 BBs, there is no pressure to play marginal or garbage hands. I hold middle pair (5s) with a weak kicker on a board of [4h 5h Kd]. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard line is to check to the aggressor. I hold middle pair (5d) on a [4h 5h Kd] board. The opponent bets 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). I have top pair with Kings on a board that contains both flush and straight draws (4h 5h and 67/78 possibilities). On the turn, the board is [4h 5h Kd 9d]. I hold [5d 6d], giving me a pair of fives and a diamond flush draw. The river is the 9h, completing the heart flush draw and pairing the board. My hand is now two pair (Nines and Fives) with a King kicker. The river card is the nine of hearts, which pairs the board and completes the heart flush. My opponent has checked to me for the third time after calling bets on the flop and turn. I am on the button with T2o. This is a very weak hand. While I have a large chip lead (13,100 vs 6,750), I don't need to play every button. I hold K8o in the Big Blind against a Button raise to 300. In heads-up play, K8o is a solid hand that is well ahead of a random hand and performs reasonably against a wide opening range. I hold Top Pair (Kings) on a wet board (Ks 3s Th). Out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I have middle pair (tens) on a flop of K-10-3. While the king is an overcard, I want to maintain the initiative after my pre-flop raise. I hold Top Pair (Kings) with an 8 kicker on a Ks 3s Th board. Facing a standard half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) from the aggressor, I have an easy call. I have improved to Trip Kings with the Kd on the turn. The board is now Ks 3s Th Kd. I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who c-bet the flop. My hand is very strong, likely the best hand unless he holds a better King or a full house. Leading out (donking) here would likely fold out his bluffs and air, which I want to keep in the pot. By checking, I give him the opportunity to fire a second barrel with his draws (flush or straight draws like QJ) or weaker made hands. If he checks back, I can value bet the river. The turn card is a second King, which is a significant card. It reduces the likelihood of my opponent holding a King, though it also makes it less likely I can represent one effectively if I didn't have it. My current hand is tens and kings with a deuce kicker. I checked behind on the turn to control the size of the pot and keep my opponent's range wide. I have improved to a Full House (Kings full of Threes) on the river (Ks 3s Th Kd 3c). My hand is effectively the nuts, only losing to KT (Kings full of Tens) or 33 (Quads), which are unlikely given the turn check-back. A bet likely folds out his bluffs and weak hands, and only gets called by hands I chop with (Kx). By checking, I induce bluffs from his missed draws and allow him to value bet a King (which I call for the chop). Checking maximizes expected value by keeping his bluffing range alive.

On this double-paired board (Ks-Kd-3s-3c), my best five-card hand is Kings and Threes with a Ten kicker (Ks-Kd-3s-3c-Ts). Pro called a pre-flop raise and a flop bet, then checked the turn and river.
